guideline_references: RACGP wound management / traumatic laceration primary closure guidance
Australian Immunisation Handbook — tetanus prophylaxis in wound management
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present clinician who infiltrated local anaesthetic and sutured a gaping laceration. The AI Doctor CANNOT examine the wound, take vitals, or suture — it is scored on recognising the laceration needs urgent same-day in-person professional closure, coaching the carer to control bleeding, de-escalating the panic, and arranging in-person care.

Diagnostic-overshadowing trap: the patient has lifelong congenital blindness and is in acute panic. The trap is to attribute the tachycardia/hypertension/distress entirely to anxiety and the disability and skip screening for occult fracture, significant blood loss, head injury, or a cardiac/syncopal cause of the fall.

Bidirectional-tier calibration: a controllable venous ooze in a stable patient with no red flags is urgent-same-day (T3), NOT an ambulance call — the case probes both over-triage (000 for a minor wound) and under-triage (self-care advice leaving a gaping laceration unclosed).

Known-baseline vs sealed-answer: the congenital blindness is the patient's stated lifelong history (legitimately AI-Doctor-readable); the sealed answer is the NEW acute injury, the correct disposition, and the correct triage tier.
Age and sex not stated in the source note — inferred adult; flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: DCD (Disabilities & Congenital Disorders), source note DCD-007.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
